result.markdown:
--- title: "Phase 5 — Covariate Model (Body Weight)" subtitle: "Allometric weight effect on clearance and volume" author: "Pramod BR" date: today format: html: theme: cosmo toc: true toc-depth: 3 code-fold: true number-sections: true fig-width: 8 fig-height: 6 execute: echo: true warning: false message: false --- ## Objectives The IIV model (Phase 4) left between-subject variability as purely *random*. Here we test whether **body weight** explains part of it, by adding an **allometric** weight effect on clearance and volume. The questions: does it improve the fit, and does the random IIV on CL/V shrink? ## How weight enters the model We use the pharmacometric-standard **allometric** form, normalised to a 70 kg reference, with the exponents estimated from the data (started at the theoretical 0.75 for CL and 1.0 for V): $$ CL = CL_{typ}\,(WT/70)^{\theta_{CL}}, \qquad V = V_{typ}\,(WT/70)^{\theta_{V}} $$ ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="false"} print(cov_model) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` function () { ini({ tka <- log(1.5) tcl <- log(2.8) tv <- log(32) dWTCL <- 0.75 dWTV <- 1 eta.ka ~ 0.3 eta.cl ~ 0.1 eta.v ~ 0.1 add.sd <- 0.2 prop.sd <- 0.2 }) model({ ka <- exp(tka + eta.ka) cl <- exp(tcl + eta.cl) * (WT/70)^dWTCL v <- exp(tv + eta.v) * (WT/70)^dWTV d/dt(depot) <- -ka * depot d/dt(center) <- ka * depot - (cl/v) * center cp <- center/v cp ~ add(add.sd) + prop(prop.sd) }) } ``` ::: ::: ## Fit the covariate model ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} dat <- build_nonmem_data() iiv_fit <- load_iiv_fit() cov_fit <- fit_cov_model(dat) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` calculating covariance matrix done ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} cov_fit ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} \begin{align*} {ka} & = \exp\left({tka}+{eta.ka}\right) \\ {cl} & = \exp\left({tcl}+{eta.cl}\right) {\times} {\left(\frac{{WT}}{{70}}\right)}^{{dWTCL}} \\ {v} & = \exp\left({tv}+{eta.v}\right) {\times} {\left(\frac{{WT}}{{70}}\right)}^{{dWTV}} \\ \frac{d \: depot}{dt} & = -{ka} {\times} {depot} \\ \frac{d \: center}{dt} & = {ka} {\times} {depot}-\left(\frac{{cl}}{{v}}\right) {\times} {center} \\ {cp} & = \frac{{center}}{{v}} \\ {cp} & \sim add({add.sd})+prop({prop.sd}) \end{align*} ::: ::: ## Parameter estimates The two new parameters are **dWTCL** and **dWTV** — the weight exponents. Their 95% confidence intervals tell us whether the effect is distinguishable from zero (no effect). ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} base_param_table(cov_fit) |> kbl(caption = "Covariate model estimates (note dWTCL, dWTV exponents)", digits = 3) |> kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"), full_width = FALSE) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Covariate model estimates (note dWTCL, dWTV exponents)
Parameter Est. SE %RSE Back-transformed(95%CI) BSV(CV%) Shrink(SD)%
tka 0.398 0.243 61.1 1.49 (0.924, 2.4) 72.3 2.32%<
tcl 1.03 0.0325 3.16 2.79 (2.62, 2.98) 26.1 3.32%<
tv 3.47 0.0984 2.84 32.1 (26.5, 39) 10.9 22.1%=
dWTCL 0.349 0.525 151 0.349 (-0.68, 1.38)
dWTV 0.586 0.273 46.6 0.586 (0.0505, 1.12)
add.sd 0.265 0.265
prop.sd 0.134 0.134
````` ::: ::: ## Is the weight effect justified? ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} cmp <- compare_models( model_metrics(iiv_fit, "IIV (no covariate)"), model_metrics(cov_fit, "Weight on CL & V") ) cmp |> kbl(caption = "IIV vs covariate model", digits = 2) |> kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"), full_width = FALSE) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
IIV vs covariate model
Model OFV AIC BIC nPar dOFV dAIC
IIV (no covariate) 104.36 362.96 386.02 8 0.00 0.00
Weight on CL & V 100.71 363.31 392.14 10 -3.65 0.35
````` ::: ::: Adding two parameters is significant (p < 0.05) if the OFV drops by more than **5.99**. Here ΔOFV = **-3.65**, so on the likelihood-ratio test the weight effect is **not clearly supported** in this dataset. ## Did the random IIV shrink? If weight genuinely drives part of the between-subject variability, the BSV(CV%) on CL and V should be smaller in the covariate model than in the IIV model. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} dplyr::full_join( iiv_cv(iiv_fit) |> dplyr::rename(`IIV model` = `BSV CV%`), iiv_cv(cov_fit) |> dplyr::rename(`Covariate model` = `BSV CV%`), by = "Parameter" ) |> kbl(caption = "Between-subject CV% before vs after adding weight", digits = 1) |> kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"), full_width = FALSE) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Between-subject CV% before vs after adding weight
Parameter IIV model Covariate model
tka 68.8 72.3
tcl 25.7 26.1
tv 12.6 10.9
````` ::: ::: ## Residual random effects vs weight After including weight, the random effects on CL and V should show little residual trend with weight — a flat line means the covariate has absorbed the size effect. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} cov_plot_eta_wt(cov_fit, dat) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Random effects on CL and V vs body weight in the covariate model.](05_Covariate_Model_Report_files/figure-html/eta-wt-1.png){width=100%} ::: ::: ## Key takeaways - Body weight was added **allometrically** on CL and V, with estimated exponents. - On the likelihood-ratio test the effect was **not clearly supported** (ΔOFV = -3.65). - We reported the change in random CV% and the residual η–weight trend honestly — in a small, weight-dosed dataset like Theoph a strong weight signal is not guaranteed, and recognising that is part of sound covariate analysis. > Next: **Phase 6 — Model diagnostics**: goodness-of-fit plots, CWRES vs > TIME/PRED, and a visual predictive check (VPC) on the selected model. ```
